workouts.description: Easy conversational run. Focus on steady breathing and relaxed form.
Easy run. Walk breaks OK if needed — the goal is time on feet.
Rest day
Long run at easy pace. No pressure on speed — just finish feeling good.

weeks.notes: Week 1: Establishing a baseline. Three runs at comfortable effort to assess current fitness.

workouts.description: Easy run with gentle 5% distance increase.
Easy run. Focus on cadence — aim for 170+ steps per minute.
Rest day
Long run progressing gently. Hydrate well beforehand.

weeks.notes: Week 2: Small distance bump. Aerobic system is adapting — keep all runs easy.

workouts.description: Easy aerobic run.
Easy run at slightly firmer pace.
Rest day
Longest run yet. You're already past 5K distance — great confidence builder.

weeks.notes: Week 3: Peak of the first base block. Long run now exceeds race distance.

workouts.description: Very easy recovery run. Go slower than you think you need to.
Short easy run. Take walk breaks if legs feel heavy.
Rest day
Reduced long run. Body is absorbing the last 3 weeks of training.

weeks.notes: Recovery week: 25% volume reduction. This is where fitness actually improves — your body adapts during rest, not during hard effort.

workouts.description: Easy run to restart after recovery week.
5 min warm-up, 15 min at comfortably hard effort (can speak short phrases), 5 min cool-down.
Rest day
Long run back to pre-recovery distance.

weeks.notes: Build week 1: First tempo session. This teaches your body to clear lactate and run at a sustained harder effort.

workouts.description: Easy recovery run after yesterday's workout.
5 min warm-up, 6x200m fast with 200m jog recovery, 5 min cool-down. Run the 200s at a pace you could hold for about 6 minutes.
Rest day
Long run. Practice race-day hydration strategy.

weeks.notes: Build week 2: First speed session. Short intervals develop neuromuscular speed and running economy.

workouts.description: Easy run.
5 min warm-up, 4x400m at goal 5K pace with 400m jog recovery, 5 min cool-down. This is race rehearsal.
Rest day
Last long run before race week.

weeks.notes: Peak week: Race-specific sharpening. The 400m intervals at goal pace teach your body exactly what race effort feels like.

workouts.description: Easy shakeout run. Legs may feel antsy — that's the taper working.
5 min warm-up, 3x200m at 5K pace with full recovery, 5 min cool-down. Stay sharp without fatiguing.
Rest day — sleep well, hydrate, lay out race gear.
RACE DAY! Start conservative, pick it up at 3K. You're ready.

weeks.notes: Taper week: 30% volume cut. You'll feel restless — that means you're fresh. Trust the training.

plan_data.supplementary_notes: Strength work: Bodyweight squats, lunges, and calf raises 2x/week on non-running days. Dynamic stretching before runs, static stretching after.
plan_data.notes: Conservative 8-week 5K plan for a beginner runner averaging 12 km/week. Follows a base-build-peak-taper periodization with a recovery week at week 4. Weekly increases stay under 10%. The plan introduces tempo and interval work only after 4 weeks of aerobic base building.

decision_log.critique: Weekly mileage increase from week 2 to week 3 exceeds 10% threshold for a conservative athlete. Long run in week 3 is 40% of weekly volume, which is fine, but the jump from 5K to 7K is too aggressive for this experience level.
issues: Week 2→3 load increase is 15%, exceeds max_weekly_increase_pct of 10%
No recovery week in first 4 weeks

decision_log.critique: Revised plan correctly includes recovery week at week 4 with 25% volume reduction. Weekly increases now stay within 10% for the conservative risk profile. Good 80/20 intensity distribution with only 2 quality sessions in the entire plan. Long run cap stays under 40%. Appropriate for a beginner.
